content.text: Handling Rules by Data Classification Tier

Classifying a prompt or output as CONFIDENTIAL is only half the work. The other half — and the part that actually reduces risk — is enforcing what happens next. Handling rules define how each sensitivity tier is treated at every stage of the data lifecycle: model routing, storage, encryption, transmission, access control, retention, audit logging, and training exclusion.

Without explicit handling rules, classification becomes a labelling exercise with no enforcement. The rules in this topic translate the five-tier taxonomy (PUBLIC → REGULATED) into concrete technical and procedural controls that can be implemented in a Gen AI pipeline and verified in an audit.

attrs.alt: Data handling rules by classification tier

content.text: The most consequential handling decision for Gen AI data is which model endpoint may process it. Routing the wrong sensitivity tier to an insufficiently controlled endpoint is the primary vector for data leakage in Gen AI deployments.
Any API provider with an active DPA
API providers with DPA + explicit training opt-out
Any provider that uses data for training by default
Private API endpoints (VPC/VNET peered) or self-hosted
Data never leaves enterprise network boundary
Certified infrastructure with BAA or FedRAMP ATO

Private endpoints (Azure OpenAI via VNet, AWS Bedrock via PrivateLink) satisfy L3 routing because traffic never traverses the public internet and the endpoint is logically isolated to the enterprise tenant. They do not satisfy L4 unless the provider also holds the relevant regulatory certification.

from enum import IntEnum
from dataclasses import dataclass

class SensitivityLevel(IntEnum):
    PUBLIC = 0; INTERNAL = 1; CONFIDENTIAL = 2
    RESTRICTED = 3; REGULATED = 4

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class ModelEndpoint:
    name: str
    max_label: SensitivityLevel   # highest tier this endpoint may process
    requires_dpa: bool
    requires_training_opt_out: bool
    network_type: str             # public / private / self_hosted

ENDPOINTS = {
    "gpt-4o-public":   ModelEndpoint("gpt-4o-public",   SensitivityLevel.INTERNAL,     True,  False, "public"),
    "gpt-4o-dpa":      ModelEndpoint("gpt-4o-dpa",      SensitivityLevel.CONFIDENTIAL, True,  True,  "public"),
    "gpt-4o-private":  ModelEndpoint("gpt-4o-private",  SensitivityLevel.RESTRICTED,   True,  True,  "private"),
    "llama-selfhosted":ModelEndpoint("llama-selfhosted", SensitivityLevel.REGULATED,    False, False, "self_hosted"),
    "hipaa-llm":       ModelEndpoint("hipaa-llm",        SensitivityLevel.REGULATED,    True,  True,  "self_hosted"),
}

def select_endpoint(label: SensitivityLevel,
                    preferred: str | None = None) -> ModelEndpoint:
    """Return the preferred endpoint if it can handle the label,
    else the least-privileged eligible endpoint."""
    if preferred and preferred in ENDPOINTS:
        ep = ENDPOINTS[preferred]
        if ep.max_label >= label:
            return ep

    eligible = sorted(
        [ep for ep in ENDPOINTS.values() if ep.max_label >= label],
        key=lambda ep: ep.max_label.value
    )
    if not eligible:
        raise ValueError(f"No endpoint available for {label.name}")
    return eligible[0]   # least-privileged that satisfies the requirement

Storage controls determine where labeled content is persisted and how it is encrypted. The controls escalate at each tier: at L2 the enterprise controls the encryption key; at L3 the key is in an HSM the enterprise operates; at L4 the data never leaves a regulated vault.
TLS in transit; provider at rest
On-premises HSM or cloud HSM dedicated
Regulated vault (separate physical or logical boundary)
Audited individual access; DPO sign-off for bulk export
Transmission rules govern how labeled data moves between systems, users, and organisations. Each tier imposes progressively stricter controls on the channels through which content may travel.
Internal; encrypt if external by exception
Encrypted email only; read receipt required

Clipboard and screenshot blocking for L3/L4 requires endpoint DLP agents (Microsoft Purview Information Protection, Forcepoint DLP, or equivalent). These cannot be enforced purely at the API gateway level — they require agent software on the user's device.
Access control rules define who may read, write, process, and administer data at each classification tier.
Named individuals; DPO notified on access
DPO + Legal approval; DPA compliance verified

Retention rules define how long labeled content is kept; deletion rules define how it is destroyed. In Gen AI workloads, retention applies to both the stored prompt/completion pairs and the audit logs about them — these are different artifacts with different retention requirements.
Per regulation (GDPR: minimum necessary; HIPAA: 6 years)
DPO sign-off + regulator notification if required

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date
from enum import Enum

class DeletionMethod(Enum):
    STANDARD     = "standard_delete"
    CRYPTO_SHRED = "crypto_shred"
    KEY_DESTROY  = "byok_key_destroy"

@dataclass
class DeletionRequest:
    request_id: str
    requester: str
    artifact_ids: list[str]
    label: SensitivityLevel
    requested_date: date
    legal_basis: str        # e.g. "GDPR Art. 17", "data subject request"

@dataclass
class DeletionRecord:
    request_id: str
    artifact_id: str
    label: SensitivityLevel
    method: DeletionMethod
    executed_at: date
    key_id: str | None       # for crypto-shred / key-destroy methods
    confirmed_by: str
    certificate_path: str | None = None

def deletion_method(label: SensitivityLevel) -> DeletionMethod:
    if label <= SensitivityLevel.INTERNAL:
        return DeletionMethod.STANDARD
    if label <= SensitivityLevel.RESTRICTED:
        return DeletionMethod.CRYPTO_SHRED
    return DeletionMethod.KEY_DESTROY

def execute_deletion(request: DeletionRequest,
                     key_store) -> list[DeletionRecord]:
    records = []
    method = deletion_method(request.label)
    for artifact_id in request.artifact_ids:
        key_id = None
        if method in (DeletionMethod.CRYPTO_SHRED, DeletionMethod.KEY_DESTROY):
            key_id = key_store.rotate_and_delete(artifact_id)
        records.append(DeletionRecord(
            request_id=request.request_id,
            artifact_id=artifact_id,
            label=request.label,
            method=method,
            executed_at=date.today(),
            key_id=key_id,
            confirmed_by="system",
        ))
    return records

By default, many commercial LLM APIs use submitted prompts and completions to improve their models. For Gen AI enterprise deployments, training exclusion must be enforced at both the contractual and technical levels for any data at L1 or above.
DPA audit + API call log verification
Required — use private endpoints that contractually guarantee exclusion
Required — use self-hosted; data never reaches provider
Infrastructure audit; no outbound traffic to provider

Audit logs for Gen AI data must capture more than access events. They must record the classification decision, the enforcement action taken, and the storage destination — enough to reconstruct the data lifecycle for any artifact.

Handling rules are occasionally too restrictive for a legitimate business need — an executive who needs to send a RESTRICTED document via email for an emergency negotiation, or a researcher who needs temporary access to REGULATED data. Exceptions must be structured to be auditable and time-bounded.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date, timedelta
from enum import Enum

class ExceptionStatus(Enum):
    PENDING  = "pending"
    APPROVED = "approved"
    DENIED   = "denied"
    EXPIRED  = "expired"

@dataclass
class HandlingException:
    exception_id: str
    requestor_id: str
    artifact_id: str
    label: SensitivityLevel
    requested_override: str     # e.g. "transmit via email", "access without ACL"
    business_justification: str
    requested_by: date
    expires_by: date
    approved_by: str | None = None
    status: ExceptionStatus = ExceptionStatus.PENDING

    def is_valid_today(self) -> bool:
        return (self.status == ExceptionStatus.APPROVED and
                self.expires_by >= date.today())

    def required_approvers(self) -> list[str]:
        if self.label == SensitivityLevel.CONFIDENTIAL:
            return ["data_owner"]
        if self.label == SensitivityLevel.RESTRICTED:
            return ["data_owner", "ciso"]
        return ["data_owner", "ciso", "dpo"]   # REGULATED

    def max_duration_days(self) -> int:
        return {
            SensitivityLevel.CONFIDENTIAL: 30,
            SensitivityLevel.RESTRICTED:   7,
            SensitivityLevel.REGULATED:    3,
        }.get(self.label, 1)
Implementing Handling Rules as Policy Objects

Handling rules are most maintainable when encoded as policy objects that can be evaluated, tested, and versioned independently of the pipeline code. This allows the data governance team to update a handling rule without a code deployment.

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class HandlingPolicy:
    label: SensitivityLevel
    permitted_model_tiers: frozenset[str]   # "any" / "dpa_covered" / "private" / "self_hosted"
    storage_tier: str
    transmission_allowed_channels: frozenset[str]
    requires_mfa: bool
    clipboard_blocked: bool
    training_excluded: bool
    min_tls_version: str
    retention_years: int | None             # None = "per regulation"
    deletion_method: str

HANDLING_POLICIES: dict[SensitivityLevel, HandlingPolicy] = {
    SensitivityLevel.PUBLIC: HandlingPolicy(
        label=SensitivityLevel.PUBLIC,
        permitted_model_tiers=frozenset(["any"]),
        storage_tier="public_cache",
        transmission_allowed_channels=frozenset(["email", "chat", "api", "web"]),
        requires_mfa=False, clipboard_blocked=False, training_excluded=False,
        min_tls_version="1.2", retention_years=1, deletion_method="standard",
    ),
    SensitivityLevel.INTERNAL: HandlingPolicy(
        label=SensitivityLevel.INTERNAL,
        permitted_model_tiers=frozenset(["any"]),  # with DPA
        storage_tier="public_cache",
        transmission_allowed_channels=frozenset(["internal_email", "internal_chat", "api"]),
        requires_mfa=True, clipboard_blocked=False, training_excluded=True,
        min_tls_version="1.2", retention_years=3, deletion_method="standard",
    ),
    SensitivityLevel.CONFIDENTIAL: HandlingPolicy(
        label=SensitivityLevel.CONFIDENTIAL,
        permitted_model_tiers=frozenset(["dpa_covered"]),
        storage_tier="encrypted_store",
        transmission_allowed_channels=frozenset(["internal_email", "api"]),
        requires_mfa=True, clipboard_blocked=False, training_excluded=True,
        min_tls_version="1.3", retention_years=7, deletion_method="crypto_shred",
    ),
    SensitivityLevel.RESTRICTED: HandlingPolicy(
        label=SensitivityLevel.RESTRICTED,
        permitted_model_tiers=frozenset(["private"]),
        storage_tier="encrypted_store",
        transmission_allowed_channels=frozenset(["encrypted_email"]),
        requires_mfa=True, clipboard_blocked=True, training_excluded=True,
        min_tls_version="1.3", retention_years=7, deletion_method="crypto_shred",
    ),
    SensitivityLevel.REGULATED: HandlingPolicy(
        label=SensitivityLevel.REGULATED,
        permitted_model_tiers=frozenset(["self_hosted"]),
        storage_tier="regulated_vault",
        transmission_allowed_channels=frozenset(["regulated_secure_channel"]),
        requires_mfa=True, clipboard_blocked=True, training_excluded=True,
        min_tls_version="1.3", retention_years=None, deletion_method="key_destroy",
    ),
}

def get_policy(label: SensitivityLevel) -> HandlingPolicy:
    return HANDLING_POLICIES[label]
Handling rules translate classification labels into enforceable controls at every stage of the Gen AI data lifecycle. Key principles:
is the highest-stakes control — wrong routing sends sensitive data to an endpoint without contractual protection.
escalates from shared cache (L0/L1) to regulated vault (L4), with encryption key control moving progressively to the enterprise.
block external channels progressively: email is DLP-filtered at L1, encrypted-only at L3, and prohibited at L4 except via regulated channels.
is required at L1 and above; for L4, self-hosted deployment is the only compliant approach.
depth increases with tier: L0 logs nothing; L4 logs every event to an immutable, SIEM-integrated, DPO-notified trail.
separate governance decisions from pipeline code, enabling the data governance team to update rules without engineering deployments.
are time-bounded, require tier-appropriate approvers, and are logged to the same audit trail as the standard access events.

The highest-risk gap in most enterprise Gen AI deployments is not classification accuracy — it is the absence of enforced handling rules. A correctly classified REGULATED prompt routed to a public API endpoint is a compliance failure; classification without enforcement solves nothing.
